title: Onsemi to acquire Synaptics for $7B in all-stock edge-AI bet
subtitle: Power-and-sensing chipmaker Onsemi is buying Synaptics for around $7 billion to fold its edge-AI compute, human-machine interface, and wireless connectivity portfolios into a single physical-AI stack — closing targeted for mid-2027.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Power-and-sensing semiconductor maker Onsemi announced on June 25 that it will acquire Synaptics in an all-stock transaction with an enterprise value of roughly $7 billion. The fixed exchange ratio is 1.350 Onsemi shares for each Synaptics share, representing about a 19% premium to both companies' recent volume-weighted average closing prices. Onsemi expects $200 million in annual synergies within 18 months of closing and projects the deal to be accretive to non-GAAP EPS on the same timeline. Closing is targeted for mid-2027.

The thesis is edge AI — running model inference on the device rather than calling out to a data center. Synaptics brings differentiated compute, human-machine interface, and wireless connectivity silicon used in cameras, hearables, automotive interiors, and consumer electronics. Onsemi already supplies the power conversion, image sensors, and silicon-carbide MOSFETs that those devices need. Combining the two gives Onsemi a stack that can sell into a single design win — sensing, power, compute, and connectivity — instead of competing slot-by-slot on the bill of materials. Onsemi told investors the combined company can address a market $30 billion larger than its standalone position, reaching $243 billion by 2030.

This is part of a broader consolidation wave around physical AI — robotics, automotive autonomy, smart appliances, industrial sensing — where the compute has to live next to the actuator rather than in a hyperscaler region. It comes a week after Qualcomm announced its $3.9 billion Modular acquisition and continues talks to acquire Tenstorrent for $8-10 billion. The shape is consistent: legacy chipmakers buying their way into the AI inference layer that everyone now expects to run at the edge. The data-center training boom that drove Nvidia past $4 trillion has a downstream cousin, and the consolidating buyers are betting it lands at industrial volume in 2027 and 2028.

A takeaway for learners: the AI hardware story is no longer just about GPUs in racks. The next wave of jobs in AI hardware sits closer to the device — embedded systems engineering, model quantization, on-device inference optimization, the boring-but-critical work of fitting a transformer into 8 watts. If you are early-career and looking for a less-crowded angle than 'work at a frontier lab,' the edge-AI design houses being rolled up right now are hiring exactly that skill set.
